source_metadata.title_raw: Keith F. Collins - A-18-248 - December 19, 2018 - Fullerton

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 19, 2018

Keith F. Collins
Jones & Mayer
3777 N. Harbor Blvd
Fullerton, CA 92835

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-248

Dear Mr. Collins:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May a councilmember who loses an election participate in a vote to appoint himself to a vacant seat on the same City Council prior to the end of his tenure?

CONCLUSION

No. A councilmember may not participate in a vote to appoint himself to a vacant seat on the same City Council prior to the end of his tenure as it is reasonably foreseeable the decision will have a material financial effect on the official's financial interest in his personal finances.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

Your office serves as City Attorney for the City of La Habra and you seek advice on behalf of councilmember Michael Blazey. You inquire whether a sitting councilmember, such as councilmember Blazey, who loses an election may nonetheless participate in a vote to appoint himself to a vacant seat² on the same City Council prior to the end of his tenure. Your request centers on the materiality provisions of Regulation 18702.5 concerning personal finances.

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² A different councilmember may forfeit his seat as a result of his election to a different body.

File No. A-18-248
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

Regulation 18702.5(a) states that the financial effect on an official's personal finances, or
those of his or her immediate family, is material if the official or immediate family member will
receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision. In turn, Regulation 18702.5(b)
currently sets forth six exceptions to this rule to make sure certain payments are not included in the
personal financial effect examination.

You have asked whether the exception in Regulation 18702.5(b)(3) is potentially applicable
to the present situation. It states that a personal financial effect does not include:

Stipends received for attendance at meetings of any group or body created
by law or formed by the official's agency for a special purpose, so long as
the selecting body posts on its website a form provided by the Commission
that includes: a list of each appointed position eligible for a stipend, the
amount of the stipend for each position, the name of the public official who
has been appointed to the position, the name of the public official, if any,
who has been appointed as an alternate, and the term of the position.

Under Regulation 18702.5, the determinative question is whether the City Council is a body
created by law or formed by the official's agency for a special purpose. However, while the City
Council is a body created by law, it is not a body created for a special purpose as intended by
Regulation 18702.5. As explained below, an appointment to the vacant position on the same City
Council prior to the end of the official's tenure was never the intent of that exception.

In 2012, the FPPC amended former 18705.2³ to allow members of city councils (or other
boards of which an official is a member) to participate in their own appointments to governing
boards of other entities formed for special purposes even though they may receive compensation or
expense reimbursement of $250 or more. Prior to 2012, such participation could have violated the
personal finances provisions because receiving compensation or reimbursement of $250 or more
within any 12-month period for service on a board would be considered "material" to the personal
finances of the official.⁴ The changes were made in recognition of the fact that, under various
provisions of state and local law, city councils and other bodies are frequently required to make
appointments from their membership to the governing boards of other entities.

Thus, the provisions in current Regulation 18702.5 were intended to allow a public official
to participate in a governmental decision to appoint himself or herself to a position in which the
public official will become an officer of the body to which the public official already belongs (e.g.,
vice mayor or mayor) (Regulation 18702.5(b)(6)), or to an outside committee, board or commission
of a public agency, a special district, a joint powers agency or authority, a joint powers insurance

³ Note that current Regulation 18702.5 was formerly Regulation 18705.5 before being renumbered in 2015.

⁴ In January 2015, the Commission removed the $250 threshold and replaced it with the current standard of
"measurable financial benefit or loss" which is determined on a case-by-case basis.

File No. A-18-248
Page No. 3

agency or authority, or a metropolitan planning organization when that appointment is required by
law or a joint powers agreement (Regulation 18702.5(b)(3)).5

Although the regulatory language of the exceptions concerning appointments has since been
amended, subsequent amendments do not alter the legal effect or purposes of Regulation
18702.5(b)(3). Thus, 18702.5(b)(3) never contemplated, nor was it ever intended to allow, a sitting
councilmember who loses an election to nonetheless participate in a vote to appoint himself to a
vacant seat on the same city council prior to the end of his tenure.6 That being the case, it is
reasonably foreseeable that voting to appoint himself to a vacant seat would have a material
financial effect on the councilmember's personal finances thereby resulting in a conflict of interest.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

5 Former Regulation 18705.2 was amended again that same year to permit officials to participate in their own
appointments to standing or ad hoc committee of the public agency of which the public official is a member.

6 Although your request does not inquire about the applicability of Regulation 18702.5(b)(6), for the same
reasons mentioned, it too would not allow such a result. That provision does not contemplate a decision to appoint an
official to the actual body on which he or she already sits; rather, it contemplates a decision to merely select a sitting
member of the body to a position on the body (such as filling a chair or officer position on a board). (See McDonald
Advice Letter, No. A-17-045.)

sections.question:
May a councilmember who loses an election participate in a vote to appoint himself to a 
vacant seat on the same City Council prior to the end of his tenure?
sections.conclusion:
No. A councilmember may not participate in a vote to appoint himself to a vacant seat on 
the same City Council prior to the end of his tenure as it is reasonably foreseeable the decision will 
have a material financial effect on the official's financial interest in his personal finances.

himself to a vacant seat2 on the same City Council prior to the end of his tenure. Your request 
centers on the materiality provisions of Regulation 18702.5 concerning personal finances. 
The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contai_ned in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 A different councilmember may forfeit his seat as a result of his election to a different body.
